Idiomatic Expressions

  • 姬不之顧 - Concise expression meaning 'the lady did not look at him' or 'the lady did not notice him.' Uses the Classical Chinese construction where the verb '顧' (to look) is followed by an object ('之' referring to the male protagonist) and the particle '不' (not).
  • 而生亦不以為帶薢也 - Can be interpreted as 'and he also did not consider it a burden' or 'and he also did not regard it as a matter of consequence.' The word '帶薢' (dàixiè) is somewhat obscure, but likely refers to a minor or inconsequential matter, possibly related to carrying a burden (帶) or a small issue (薢).
  • 明府某公 - Respectful title for a local official, often used in Classical Chinese to address a governor or magistrate. '明府' (míngfǔ) is a formal honorific, and '某公' (mǒngōng) is a placeholder for the official's name.
  • 任侠好义 - Compound term describing someone who is a 'champion of justice' or 'upholder of righteousness.' '任侠' (rènxiá) refers to someone who takes on the role of a righteous avenger, while '好义' (hǎoyì) means 'loves righteousness.'
  • 素与生友善 - Phrase meaning 'has always been friendly with the protagonist.' '素' (sù) indicates a long-standing relationship, and '友善' (yǒushàn) means 'friendly.'
  • 以爱生才而怜其太痴 - Can be translated as 'because he admired the protagonist's talent and pitied his excessive naivety' or 'because he valued the protagonist's abilities and felt sorry for his foolishness.' '爱生才' (ài shēng cái) means 'admired the protagonist's talent,' and '怜其太痴' (lián qí tài chī) means 'pitied his excessive naivety.'
  • 愿出千金为姬脱籍 - Phrase meaning 'willing to spend a thousand gold coins to free the lady from her registered status.' '愿出' (yuàn chū) means 'willing to spend,' '千金' (qiānjīn) is a large sum of money, and '为姬脱籍' (wèi jī tuōjí) means 'to free the lady from her registered status.'
  • 生大喜 - Simple phrase meaning 'the protagonist was very happy.' '大喜' (dàxǐ) means 'very happy.'
  • 商之翁媪 - Phrase meaning 'consulted with the old man and woman.' '商之' (shāng zhī) means 'consulted,' and '翁媪' (wēng ǎo) refers to the elderly couple.
  • 诺之 - Phrase meaning 'agreed to it.' '诺' (nuò) is a verb meaning 'to agree' or 'to promise.'
  • 转以问姬 - Phrase meaning 'then turned to ask the lady.' '转以问' (zhuǎn yǐ wèn) means 'turned to ask,' and '姬' (jī) refers to the lady.
  • 抵死不肯 - Phrase meaning 'refused stubbornly' or 'refused absolutely.' '抵死' (dǐ sǐ) is an emphatic expression meaning 'stubbornly' or 'absolutely,' and '不肯' (bù kěn) means 'refused.'
  • 说之再三 - Phrase meaning 'persuaded her repeatedly.' '说之' (shuō zhī) means 'persuaded,' and '再三' (zài sān) means 'repeatedly.'
  • 始勉强应诺 - Phrase meaning 'finally reluctantly agreed.' '始' (shǐ) means 'finally,' '勉强' (miǎnqiǎng) means 'reluctantly,' and '应诺' (yìngnuò) means 'agreed.'
  • 并与翁媪约 - Phrase meaning 'and made an agreement with the old man and woman.' '并' (bìng) means 'and,' '与' (yǔ) means 'with,' and '约' (yuē) means 'agreement.'
  • 亲迎之次日即归宁 - Phrase meaning 'on the day after the wedding, she would return to her parents' home.' '亲迎' (qīnyíng) refers to the wedding ceremony, '次日' (cìrì) means 'the next day,' and '归宁' (guīníng) means 'return to her parents' home.'
  • 凡舟中己之床奁什物 - Phrase meaning 'all the beds, trunks, and household items in the boat.' '凡' (fán) means 'all,' '舟中' (zhōuzhōng) means 'in the boat,' '己之' (jǐ zhī) means 'her own,' and '床奁什物' (chuánglián shíwù) refers to personal belongings.
  • 毋许动移 - Phrase meaning 'do not allow them to be moved.' '毋' (wú) is a negative particle, '许' (xǔ) means 'allow,' and '动移' (dòngyí) means 'move.'
  • 叮嘱谆谆 - Phrase meaning 'repeatedly and earnestly instructed.' '叮嘱' (dīngzhǔ) means 'instructed,' and '谆谆' (zhūnzhūn) is an adverb meaning 'repeatedly' or 'earnestly.'
  • 极口许诺 - Phrase meaning 'vigorously agreed.' '极口' (jíkǒu) means 'vigorously' or 'with all one's mouth,' and '许诺' (xǔnuò) means 'agreed.'
  • 然后兑金署券 - Phrase meaning 'then exchanged the money and signed the contract.' '然后' (ránhòu) means 'then,' '兑金' (duìjīn) means 'exchanged the money,' and '署券' (shǔquàn) means 'signed the contract.'
  • 薄暮 - Phrase meaning 'late afternoon' or 'dusk.' '薄暮' (bómù) is a classical expression for late afternoon.
  • 城门已鐍 - Phrase meaning 'the city gates were already locked.' '城门' (chéngmén) means 'city gates,' and '已鐍' (yǐ jué) means 'already locked.' The character '鐍' (jué) is an archaic form of '锁' (suǒ), meaning 'locked.'
  • 足音杳然 - Phrase meaning 'her footsteps vanished' or 'her presence disappeared.' '足音' (zúyīn) means 'footsteps,' and '杳然' (yǎorán) means 'vanished' or 'disappeared.'
  • 竟夜徘徊 - Phrase meaning 'spent the entire night wandering.' '竟夜' (jìngyè) means 'throughout the night,' and '徘徊' (páihuái) means 'wandering.'
  • 起坐太息 - Phrase meaning 'got up and sighed deeply.' '起坐' (qǐzuò) means 'got up and sat down,' and '太息' (tàixī) means 'sighed deeply.'
  • 目不交睫 - Phrase meaning 'did not close his eyes even for a moment.' '目不交睫' (mùbùjiāojié) is a classical expression meaning 'did not close his eyes,' where '睫' (jié) refers to eyelashes.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 姬 - Refers to a concubine or妾 (qiè), a woman of lower status than a wife but higher than a servant. In this context, '姬' likely refers to a woman in a relationship with the protagonist, possibly a concubine or a lover.
  • 明府 - Respectful title for a local official, often used in Classical Chinese to address a governor or magistrate. This reflects the hierarchical and formal nature of Chinese society during this period.
  • 任侠好义 - This phrase reflects the cultural ideal of the '侠客' (xiákè), a person who champions justice and righteousness, often associated with the Warring States period and the Han dynasty. The concept of '侠' (xiá) is deeply embedded in Chinese literature and philosophy, representing a code of honor and loyalty.
  • 脱籍 - Refers to the process of freeing a woman from her registered status as a concubine or a woman under the control of another family. This practice was common in ancient China, especially in cases where a woman wanted to leave her current situation and start a new life.
  • 亲迎 - Refers to the traditional Chinese wedding ceremony where the groom personally goes to the bride's home to bring her back to his family. This ritual was an important part of Chinese marriage customs and symbolized the union of two families.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 入城見姬，責其背逃之罪 - This clause introduces the scene and the accusation, with '責其背逃之罪' being a clear statement of the charge.
  • 嫁夫隨夫，何謂背逃 - This is a rhetorical question, where '嫁夫隨夫' sets up the expectation that following one's husband is normal, and '何謂背逃' challenges the accusation of fleeing.
  • 翁姆尚念舊好，請勿贅言，後日尚可往來；不則從此斬斷葛藤，兩為陌路矣 - This clause presents a conditional statement, offering two possible outcomes: continued friendship or severing ties.
  • 翁媪以姬明決，悔恨之極 - This clause describes the old couple's reaction, with '以姬明決' indicating their perception of Lady J's decisiveness and '悔恨之極' expressing their deep regret.
  • 欲訴官，以前既憑媒署券，後又經郡守判斷，更難翻覆 - This clause explains the legal situation, with '欲訴官' indicating the intention to sue, and '更難翻覆' emphasizing the difficulty of overturning the judgment.
  • 乃白眼瞪視，垂頭嘿嘆者久之，不得已，甘言強笑，訂盟而別 - This clause describes the old couple's behavior, with '白眼瞪視' and '垂頭嘿嘆' indicating their contempt and regret, and '甘言強笑，訂盟而別' showing their reluctant agreement.
  • 蓋姬平日私蓄，固有萬餘金，嫁生斷難攜帶，必如此作為，使翁媪不覺，然後兩舟相並便好陸續攜運，若稍露聲色，則防察必嚴，絲毫莫取矣 - This long clause explains Lady J's plan, with '蓋姬平日私蓄' indicating her secret savings, '嫁生斷難攜帶' explaining the difficulty of carrying it all, and '必如此作為' detailing her strategy.
  • 其機甚警，而其心亦甚苦哉 - This clause comments on Lady J's cleverness and the bitterness of her situation.
  • 姬尋出金為生納資縣令，所在悉著政聲，皆由內助之力居多焉 - This clause concludes the passage, with '姬尋出金為生納資縣令' indicating Lady J's action, '所在悉著政聲' describing her husband's reputation, and '皆由內助之力居多焉' attributing it to her support.

  • 嗟乎！风尘莽莽，豪杰尽多，俗眼不识，则交臂失之者不知凡几，惜哉！ - This sentence is a rhetorical exclamation, emphasizing the regret of not recognizing many heroes in the world.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 祝由科 - A specialized medical technique that does not use drugs but instead uses rituals, prayers, and symbols (符) to cure diseases.
  • 按脈能知人七十二經 - Literally means 'by feeling the pulse, one can know the 72 meridians of a person.' This refers to the ability to diagnose through pulse reading, which is a common practice in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 投以藥，无不效 - Literally means 'when given medicine, it is always effective.' This emphasizes the effectiveness of the treatment.
  • 勅勒书符水面 - Literally means 'to inscribe a talisman on the water surface with a command.' This describes the ritual act of writing a symbol on water, which is believed to have healing properties.
  • 誓不受钱币之谢 - Literally means 'swears not to accept monetary thanks.' This indicates that the practitioner does not accept money as payment.
  • 或酬以酒食则可耳 - Literally means 'or accept payment in the form of alcohol and food.' This suggests that while money is not accepted, other forms of gratitude are acceptable.
  • 擅此术者虽多，而真得秘传者甚少 - Literally means 'although many people possess this skill, those who truly receive the secret transmission are very few.' This highlights the rarity of genuine mastery.
  • 起死回生之功 - Literally means 'the power to bring the dead back to life.' This is a hyperbolic way of describing the effectiveness of the technique.
  • 伪托其名以欺人 - Literally means 'to falsely claim the name to deceive people.' This refers to practitioners who pretend to have the skill without actually possessing it.
  • 愚夫愚妇受其欺者 - Literally means 'foolish men and women who are deceived by them.' This describes the victims of fraudulent practitioners.
  • 坐失巨资 - Literally means 'sitting and losing a large amount of money.' This emphasizes the financial loss suffered by the victims.
  • 悔之无及矣 - Literally means 'regretting it is too late.' This indicates that the victims cannot undo the damage done.
  • 每岁元旦祭祷祝由之神 - Literally means 'every year on the first day of the first month, they worship and pray to the god of Zhubu.' This describes the annual ritual performed by genuine practitioners.
  • 默占以笤 - Literally means 'to silently predict using a broom.' This refers to a method of divination used to predict the number of patients they will treat in the coming year.
  • 最为灵验 - Literally means 'most effective and accurate.' This emphasizes the reliability of the prediction method.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 黄帝 - Refers to the legendary Yellow Emperor, a central figure in Chinese mythology and the founder of Chinese civilization.
  • 岐伯氏 - A legendary figure in Chinese medicine, often paired with the Yellow Emperor in the *Huangdi Neijing* (Yellow Emperor's Inner Canon), one of the foundational texts of traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 祝由氏 - Another legendary figure in Chinese medicine, known for his non-drug-based healing techniques.
  • 辰州府 - Refers to a historical administrative region in Hunan Province, China, known for its traditional healing practices.
  • 符 - A talisman or symbol used in traditional Chinese medicine and rituals, believed to have magical or healing properties.

Proper Nouns

  • 黄帝 - The Yellow Emperor.
  • 岐伯氏 - The legendary figure associated with traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 祝由氏 - The legendary figure associated with non-drug-based healing techniques.
  • 辰州府 - A historical administrative region in Hunan Province, China.
  • 符 - A talisman or symbol used in traditional Chinese medicine and rituals.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 曩过豫州 - In the past, the narrator visited a place called Yu Zhou.
  • 汴城节署旗竿斗损坏 - The flagpole of the high-ranking official's office in Bianjing was damaged.
  • 匠人以绳轳辘上升其巅为之整理 - The craftsman used a rope and pulley system to climb to the top and fix the flagpole.
  • 竿忽为风折 - The flagpole was suddenly broken by the wind.
  • 匠人堕为齑粉 - The craftsman fell and was reduced to powder.
  • 佥谓无再生之理 - Everyone believed there was no hope of revival.
  • 笑谓：“无患。” - The skilled practitioner smiled and said, 'There is no worry.'
  • 乃命人取板四片 - He ordered someone to fetch four boards.
  • 以一板上置黄土 - He placed yellow earth on one board.
  • 将匠人舁置土上 - He lifted and placed the craftsman on the earth.
  • 左右夹以二板 - He clamped the sides with two boards.
  • 又以黄土遍撒其身 - He sprinkled yellow earth all over his body.
  • 以手捏剑诀 - He formed a gesture used in swordplay with his hand.
  • 勅勒书符 - He wrote a talisman or inscribed a charm.
  • 口中喃喃诵咒 - He mumbled a spell.
  • 毕，复吸清水噀其面 - After finishing, he inhaled clear water and sprayed it on his face.
  • 上盖一板，以麻绳束之 - He covered with one board and tied it with hemp rope.
  • 七日后，解去麻绳，启板视之 - After seven days, he untied the hemp rope and opened the board to look at him.
  • 气热如蒸，匠人竟体大汗 - The air was hot like steam, and the craftsman sweated all over his body.
  • 欠伸而起，已霍然苏矣 - He yawned and stretched, stood up, and was completely revived.
  • 人问及前事，懵然不知 - When people asked about what happened before, he had no memory of it.
  • 但谓如梦初觉，殊无所苦 - He said it was as if waking from a dream and felt no discomfort at all.
  • 噫嘻！其术如此，真神乎技矣 - The narrator expresses admiration for the skilled practitioner's abilities.
  • 闻节署前曾泐碑详记其事，惜匆匆过此，未及访阅耳 - The narrator heard that a stone tablet had been carved in detail to record the event, but he passed by too quickly to visit and read it.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 豫州 - A place name, likely referring to a region in Henan province.
  • 汴城 - Refers to Bianjing (modern-day Kaifeng), the capital of the Song Dynasty.
  • 节署 - Refers to the office of a high-ranking official.
  • 辰州 - A place name, likely referring to Chenzhou, a city in Hunan province.
  • 术客 - A person skilled in magic or medicine.
  • 黄土 - Refers to loess, a type of soil found in northern China.
  • 剑诀 - A gesture or sign used in swordplay, often associated with martial arts and traditional Chinese culture.
  • 书符 - Writing a talisman or inscribing a charm, a common practice in traditional Chinese medicine and magic.
  • 喃喃诵咒 - Reciting a spell, a practice often associated with religious or magical rituals.
  • 噀其面 - Spraying water on the face, a ritual act that can have cleansing or healing properties.
  • 七日 - Seven days, a significant number in many cultures, often associated with completion or transformation.
  • 霍然苏 - Reviving suddenly, a term often used to describe a miraculous recovery.
  • 泐碑 - Carving a stone tablet, a common practice for recording important events or achievements in Chinese history.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The opening clause introduces the narrator's past visit and the subsequent account given by local people.
  • This clause provides specific details about the incident, including the location and the nature of the damage.
  • This clause describes the method used to repair the flagpole.
  • This clause describes the sudden and tragic outcome of the repair attempt.
  • This clause summarizes the general belief that the craftsman could not be revived.
  • This clause introduces the arrival of a skilled practitioner and his confident response.
  • This long, complex clause details the elaborate ritual performed by the skilled practitioner.
  • This clause describes the miraculous recovery of the craftsman after the ritual.
  • This clause explains the craftsman's lack of memory of the event and his feeling of no discomfort.
  • This interjection expresses admiration for the skilled practitioner's abilities.
  • This final clause notes the existence of a stone tablet that records the event in detail.
